hypothesis.hypothesis_rationale: High real estate costs (ZHVI) increase overhead costs like rent and payroll, which reduces the amount of net contributions per dollar spent on fundraising.
hypothesis.prior_art_notes: Harrison & Wolch (1998) examined spatial mismatches and operating overhead constraints for urban non-profits.

interpretation.interpretation: A highly significant negative correlation was found. For every unit increase in log ZHVI, winsorized fundraising efficiency drops by 7.92 units. This indicates operating in high-cost housing markets penalizes nonprofit efficiency.
